agent1_snippets.title: Physiology, Proteins -- Organ Systems Involved -- Diet
agent1_snippets.content: In general, animal-based protein foods such as eggs, dairy, meat, and seafood provide all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts. Soy-based foods are unique because they are tasteless and provide all nine essential amino in sufficient quantities. Most other plant foods, including whole grains, nuts, legumes, and seeds, possess high levels of some amino acids and low amounts of others. It would be wrong to assume that animal-based foods provide more protein than plant-based ones. A cup of tofu contains the same number of grams of protein as 3 ounces of steak, chicken, or fish. A half-cup of lentils has more grams of protein than an egg. Not all plant foods are low in the same amino acids, so eating various plant-based foods can provide all nine essentials. For example, pairing protein sources like rice and beans, hummus, pita bread, or oatmeal topped with almond butter. However, regarding volume, it may be necessary to eat more plant-based foods to get a similar amount of protein and amino acid profile provided by animal-based proteins. [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Butterfly, Moth, and Caterpillar Envenomation -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Other notable venomous caterpillars in the US include the flannel moth ( Megalopyge crispata ), the lo moth ( Automeris io ), the saddleback moth ( Acharia stimulat a), and the hickory tiger moth ( Lophocampa caryae ). [2] [13] Lonomia obliqua caterpillars in South America, especially in Brazil, are infamous for causing severe pain and hemorrhagic syndrome. [14] [15] In Australia, caterpillars such as the mistletoe brown-tail moth ( Euproctis edwardsi ), processionary caterpillars ( Ochrogaster lunifer ), cup moths ( Doratifera spp. ), and the white-stemmed gum moth ( Chelepteryx colles i) are of medical importance. [16] Pine processionary caterpillars ( Thaumetopoea pityocampa ) are the important defoliator of pine forests in the Mediterranean and central European countries, with significant economic and occupational consequences for workers in these forests. [17] The Anaphe venata caterpillar in Nigeria is an essential protein resource but can cause thiamine deficiency syndrome similar to dry beriberi. [18] [19] Additionally, dendrolimus caterpillars in China and Preolis semirufa caterpillars in Brazil are linked to significant joint disease. [20] [4]

agent1_snippets.title: Opsoclonus -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Emerging therapies and future research: Biologic agents targeting B-cell and T-cell pathways are promising for steroid-sparing immunotherapy. Interleukin 6 inhibitors (tocilizumab) are under investigation for refractory OMS. Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors targeting the immune cascade are being explored for chronic relapsing cases. Gene expression profiling may identify genetic susceptibility to autoimmune OMS and aid in personalized medicine approaches. [106]

agent1_snippets.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Allergenic proteins from pollen, dander, dust mites, insects, mold, among others are the main ingredient of allergen extract. However, the final product is a mixture of diluents or solvents and preservatives. Different extracts including aqueous, glycerinated, lyophilized, acetone precipitated, alum-precipitated are available. Diluents will keep the allergen in liquid form; commonly used agents are glycerin, phenol saline, and HSA. The staff should use measures that include good personal hygiene, hand washing, and antiseptics to clean working areas. These include a water-based disinfectant followed by the application of alcohol on working surfaces for preparing allergen extracts. Alcohol kills organisms by dehydration. Sanitization will prevent bacterial contamination. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Atopy -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: The list of occupational allergens causing IgE-mediated allergic asthma includes animal products (obtained from cows, pigs, mice, dogs, cats, and horses), insect dust (from mealworms, storage mites, cockroaches, bees, and flies), plant products (such as fruits, vegetables, seeds, leaves, pollens, flours, plant, grain, and cotton dust, castor beans, tobacco, and weeping fig). Other allergens include gums and extracts (such as western red, California redwood, and exotic woods), microbial agents (fungal allergens, alginates, protozoa, bacteria, and fungi), enzymes (such as papain, hog trypsin, pancreatic extracts, subtilisin, and pineapple bromelain), therapeutic agents (such as penicillins, tetracycline, cephalosporins, sulfonamides, and spiramycin), sterilizing agents (such as chloramides), inorganic chemicals (such as metal fumes and salts, aluminum, cobalt, fluoride, nickel, platinum, vanadium, and zinc) and organic chemicals (such as amines, anhydrides, and azodicarbonamide). [10]

agent1_snippets.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Allergy immunotherapy (AIT),  also referred to as allergen desensitization or hypo-sensitization was first introduced by Leonard Noon and John Freeman in 1911, they proposed that people with hay fever were sensitive to grass pollen toxins. [1] Noon is credited for developing a process involved in the extraction of timothy pollen in distilled water and then boiling it to create an extract. [1] This extract was then injected in increasing doses to alleviate symptoms in patients.  This concept is widely in use today with a modified approach. Currently, the term immunotherapy is used to describe all methods to overcome abnormal immune responses with induction of clonal deletion, anergy, immune tolerance, and immune deviation. [2] However, the term desensitization is "a method, to develop a temporary state of tolerance to an agent responsible for an allergic or hypersensitivity reaction." [3] Furthermore, immunotherapy is a disease-modifying treatment and the effects can last longer even after stopping the treatment, which then provides prophylactic effects. [3] [4] [5] [6] [7] [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Wheat Allergy -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: In cases of exposure and an anaphylactic reaction, the administration of epinephrine is the lifesaving treatment. Epinephrine comes in either a 0.15 or 0.3 mg intramuscular injection and is typically injected into the lateral thigh. All patients should go to the emergency room for further evaluation and antihistamines, glucocorticoids, and beta-agonist are additional secondary treatment options. [1] [23] Additional treatments include immunotherapy such as oral immunotherapy (OIT), sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT), and epicutaneous immunotherapy (EPIT). [1] [36] OIT and SLIT both utilize the principle of gradually increasing the amount of food ingested leading to desensitization and thus hopefully helping to avoid experiencing an allergic reaction. EPIT utilizes a skin patch to deliver an allergen to the patient. Those with WDEIA require epinephrine and can avoid symptoms by, avoiding exercising within four to six hours after wheat ingestion, avoid exercising alone or in hot weather/pollen season, and carrying emergency medication. [1] [20]

agent1_snippets.content: Finally, sublingual immunotherapy involves placing emulsified purified peanut protein under the tongue for 120 seconds and then swallowing. Data show that this can help prevent peanut allergic reactions with time.

agent1_snippets.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Asthma
agent1_snippets.content: Allergen immunotherapy can reduce short-term symptoms in allergic asthma; however, there is a moderate increase in the risk of systemic and local reactions based on a meta-analysis. [6] A 3-year course of either sublingual or subcutaneous immunotherapy prevents asthma for up to 2 years in children and adolescents with grass/birch pollen that triggers moderate to severe allergic rhinitis. However, this still requires further research. [4]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2526
agent2_snippets.content: Chaste tree preparation is described as a multi‑component medicinal formulation that appears in several dosage forms, each containing a distinct set of active ingredients or moieties. In the sublingual solution and drops marketed as “R18 OVAGEN,” the preparation lists adenosine triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, Apis mellifera, chaste tree, ammonium carbonate, and Lycopodium clavatum spore among its active ingredients. The oral pellet included in the “Joette Calabrese Survivalist Kit” is an even broader chaste tree preparation, comprising chaste tree together with a long list of botanical extracts (e.g., Artemis abrotanum, Aconitum napellus, Aethusa cynapium, Arnica montana, Atropa belladonna, Bapitisia tinctoria, Borrelia burgdorferi, Crotalus horridus venom, Daphne mezereum bark, Na ja na ja venom, Vipera berus venom, and many others), minerals (e.g., gold, barium carbonate, copper, lead, mercury compounds), acids (hydrofluoric, hydrochloric, phosphoric, nitric, picric, sulfuric), and synthetic chemicals (e.g., quinine sulfate, phenol, cadmium sulfate, bromine). In the oral spray product “OHM‑DAM,” the chaste tree preparation is defined by its active moiety, chaste tree fruit, and is combined with Avena sativa flowering top, Turnera diffusa leafy twig, Gelsemium sempervirens root, Strychnos ignatii seed, Lycopodium clavatum spore, Bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, Tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine. Across all formulations, chaste tree (whether as fruit, extract, or other part) is consistently identified as a key active component, but each product also incorporates a unique constellation of additional botanical, mineral, and chemical substances that together constitute the therapeutic profile of the respective chaste tree preparation.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
agent2_snippets.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) or corticotropin, is a peptide hormone that belongs to the broader class of amino acids, peptides, and proteins. Numerous oral liquid preparations list ACTH as an active ingredient, often under the designation “corticotropin.” These formulations combine ACTH with a wide array of additional substances, including epinephrine, various mineral salts (iodine, sodium chloride, potassium carbonate, calcium carbonate, phosphorus, arsenic trioxide, silver nitrate), vitamins (niacinamide, thiamine hydrochloride), amino acids (aspartic acid, glutamic acid), nucleic acids (Saccharomyces cerevisiae RNA, herring sperm DNA), plant extracts (Mucuna pruriens seed, Anacardium occidentale fruit, Clematis recta flowering top, Datura stramonium, Atropa belladonna, Berberis vulgaris root bark, Phytolacca americana root, Nutmeg, Anemone pulsatilla, Lyco­podium clavatum spore), animal gland extracts (suspensions of sheep thyroid, adrenal gland, pituitary gland, hypothalamus, parathyroid gland, posterior pituitary, placenta), and other bioactive compounds (insulin, adenosine triphosphate, ubidecarenone, melatonin, testosterone, aldosterone, corticorelin, prasterone). Specific product complexes—such as OHM Pituitarum Complex, FCS HP, MBM 11 Thyroid Adrenal, and HP Axis—use these multi‑component mixtures to deliver ACTH alongside agents that may support or modulate endocrine function, cardiovascular response, metabolic processes, and immune activity. The inclusion of ACTH in these diverse formulations reflects its therapeutic role in stimulating adrenal cortisol production and managing conditions related to adrenal insufficiency or other disorders of the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis.

Buprenorfin is simultaneously classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid, a plant agent product and a phytonutrient, underscoring its botanical origin. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 733
agent2_snippets.content: The chaste tree preparation is classified as a member of the “other gynecologicals in ATC” group, which is a subclass of the broader “GENITO‑URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES” category; this category in turn belongs to the “GYNECOLOGICAL ANTI‑INFECTIVES AND ANTISEPTICS” class. As a gynecological product, the preparation contains a complex mixture of active ingredients and active moieties. In one formulation, the sublingual solution/drops (R18 OVAGEN) lists adenosine‑triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, apis mellifera, chaste tree, ammonium carbonate and lycopodium clavatum spore among its active ingredients. A second oral pellet formulation (Joette Calabrese Survivalist Kit) includes an extensive array of substances such as artemisia abrotanum, aconitum napellus, chaste tree, various mineral salts (e.g., ammonium carbonate, antimony trisulfide, barium carbonate), metals (gold, copper, cadmium, cobalt, lead, mercury compounds), plant extracts (e.g., arnica montana, milk thistle, hyoscyamus niger, veratrum album), animal venoms (e.g., Bothrops atrox, Naja naja, Vipera berus), and numerous acids and organic compounds (e.g., hydrofluoric acid, nitric acid, phenol, quinine sulfate). A third formulation described as an oral spray (OHM‑DAM) identifies active moieties such as chaste tree fruit, avena sativa flowering top, turnera diffusa leafy twig, gelsemium sempervirens root, strychnos ignatii seed, lycopodium clavatum spore, bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine. Together, these details illustrate that the chaste tree preparation is a multi‑component gynecological agent, positioned within the ATC hierarchy of sex‑hormone and genitourinary therapies, and formulated with a wide spectrum of botanical, mineral, metallic, and synthetic constituents intended for therapeutic use in women’s health.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
agent2_snippets.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), is a cyclic peptide composed of the sequence glutamyl‑histidyl‑phenylalanyl‑arginyl‑tryptophyl‑glycyl‑lysyl‑prolyl‑valyl‑glycinamide. This hormone is listed as an active ingredient in several veterinary oral liquid formulations, including Neurobalance Pro, Appetite and Weight Natural Pet, Recovatone, and HP Axis. In each of these products, ACTH is combined with a wide array of additional compounds such as catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, serotonin hydrochloride), corticosteroids (cortisone acetate, corticosterone, aldosterone), other hormones (testosterone, melatonin, progesterone), minerals (potassium chloride, calcium gluconate, magnesium chloride, zinc gluconate, selenium, copper, cobalt, manganese, iron, boron, vanadium, chromium, strontium carbonate), vitamins (ascorbic acid), amino acids (arginine, ornithine, leucine, valine, glutamine, creatine), plant extracts (semecarpus anacardium juice, agrimonia eupatoria flower, castanea sativa flower, nerium oleander leaf, delphinium staphisagria seed, fucus vesiculosus, berberis vulgaris root bark, silver nitrate, etc.), and various electrolytes and salts (sodium chloride, potassium gluconate, dibasic potassium phosphate, tribasic calcium phosphate). The repeated inclusion of ACTH across these multi‑component liquids highlights its therapeutic role in modulating the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis in animal patients, often in conjunction with other endocrine, neurotransmitter, nutritional, and botanical agents to support adrenal function, metabolic balance, and overall health.

A related spray (OHM‑DAM) also lists chaste‑tree fruit together with oat (Avena sativa) flowering tops, Turnera diffusa twigs, gelsemium root, strychnos seeds, lycopodium spores, bull testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, Tribulus terrestris and yohimbine, illustrating the multi‑component nature of some herbal products. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 304
agent2_snippets.content: Chaste tree preparation is classified within the ATC subgroup “other gynecologicals,” indicating its use as a gynecological therapeutic agent. Multiple formulations of this preparation have been described, including a sublingual solution/drops (R18 OVAGEN), an oral pellet (Joette Calabrese Survivalist Kit), and an oral spray (OHM‑DAM). Across these products, chaste tree preparation consistently contains chaste tree–derived material as a principal component, whether listed as CHASTE TREE, CHASTE TREE 200, or CHASTE TREE FRUIT, and it is identified both as an active ingredient and as an active moiety. In addition to the chaste tree constituents, the preparations incorporate a wide array of other substances such as adenosine triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, apis mellifera (honeybee), ammonium carbonate, lycopodium clavatum spore, and numerous botanical, mineral, and chemical agents (e.g., artemisia abrotanum, aconitum napellus, gold, mercury compounds, various venoms, acids, and metals). These diverse components reflect the complex, multi‑ingredient nature of the formulations. Bremelanotide is also a member of the ATC category “other gynecologicals,” placing it alongside chaste tree preparation as another drug used for gynecological indications. The shared ATC classification underscores a therapeutic relationship between bremelanotide and chaste tree preparation, both being recognized as agents within the same pharmacological group for women's health.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 506
agent2_snippets.content: The oral liquid allergy formulation contains numerous biologically active substances—including adenosine cyclic phosphate, corticotropin, onion extract, various plant roots, cortisone acetate, histamine dihydrochloride, electrolytes, sulfur, and the peptide hormone adrenokortikotropní hormon (ACTH). ACTH belongs to the amino‑acid/peptide/protein class, functions as a hormone, and is linked to tumor biomarkers, making it relevant for oncologic diagnostics. Other peptide hormones in the network are erythropoietin and glucagon; glucagon is also a hormone and has a documented contraindication with the drug ورم جزيري. Digitoxin and coagulation factor VII are classified as biologically active substances, as are hormones in general. Hormones comprise several subgroups: gonadotropins, progestins (e.g., megestrolacetát), and catecholamines (e.g., karbidopa). Gonadotropins are a hormone subclass, and catecholamines are associated with tumor biomarkers, suggesting a role in cancer‑marker panels. Endocannabinoids are connected to cannabinoids, which bind cannabinoid receptors and are categorized as controlled substances; endocannabinoids also exert downstream effects on hormones. Follitropin beta is a member of the structural class gonadotropiny, the same class that includes lidský choriogonadotropin (human chorionic gonadotropin). Both lidský choriogonadotropin and normetanefrin are identified as tumor biomarkers, indicating their utility in tumor detection and monitoring. Overall, the network illustrates how peptide and protein hormones, other biologically active substances, their receptor systems, and associated tumor biomarkers intersect across therapeutic, diagnostic, and regulatory contexts.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
agent2_snippets.content: The chaste tree preparation is a multi‑ingredient oral‑spray formulation that contains adenosine triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, Apis mellifera, chaste tree fruit, ammonium carbonate, Lycopodium clavatum spore, Avena sativa flowering top, Turnera diffusa leafy twig, Gelsemium sempervirens root, Strychnos ignatii seed, Bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, Tribulus terrestris, yohimbine and numerous other botanical and mineral constituents. In the ATC system this preparation, together with flibanserin and bremelanotide, is placed in the class “Other gynecologicals”, which also includes anti‑inflammatory products for vaginal administration. Within this class the subclass “Other uterotonics” is defined as a subset of “Prostaglandins, oxytocics”, which in turn belong to the broader class “Uterotonics”; dinoprost is an example of a drug in the “Prostaglandins, oxytocics” group, linking it to uterotonic activity. Flibanserin is characterized as a P‑glycoprotein inhibitor, is contraindicated with cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers, and is chemically a benzimidazole. Bremelanotide is classified as a therapeutic hormone, a member of the therapeutic steroid‑hormone subclass that also includes oral contraceptive agents and anaritide. Ergot‑alkaloid oxytocics, a subgroup of uterotonics, include ergonovin. Direct thrombin inhibitors comprise argatroban and dabigatran etexilate; dabigatran etexilate shares the benzimidazole scaffold with lomitapide. Klobazam exemplifies a cytochrome P450 3A4 inducer. This integrated network delineates how the chaste tree preparation and various gynecological, uterotonic, hormonal, and anticoagulant agents are categorized, their chemical families, and their interactions with metabolic pathways such as P‑glycoprotein transport and CYP3A4 metabolism, informing their clinical application.

The inclusion of both synthetic and natural substances across the products underscores a multimodal approach to patient (or animal) care that leverages synergistic actions of catecholamines, ACTH, and supportive nutrients to address conditions such as allergic reactions, neuro‑behavioral imbalances, weight management, and systemic endocrine deficiencies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
agent2_snippets.content: Adrenalin, also known as epinephrine, is the parent substance of the class “Adrenal medulla hormone (substance),” reflecting its role as the primary hormone produced by the adrenal medulla. Adrenalin serves as the active ingredient in several multi‑component oral liquid formulations. In the OHM Pituitarum Complex (containing EPINEPHRINE 10 [hp_X] in 59 mL, CORTICOTROPIN 10 [hp_X] in 59 mL, SUS SCROFA CEREBRUM 14 [hp_X] in 59 mL, SUS SCROFA ADRENAL GLAND 10 [hp_X] in 59 mL, SUS SCROFA PITUITARY GLAND, POSTERIOR 10 [hp_X] in 59 mL, SUS SCROFA PLACENTA 8 [hp_X] in 59 mL, and THYROID, UNSPECIFIED 8 [hp_X] in 59 mL), adrenalin is listed as a key active component. A second oral liquid product (FCS HP) combines EPINEPHRINE 30 [hp_C] in 1 mL with a wide array of substances—including GLUTAMIC ACID, MUCUNA PRURIENS SEED, NIACINAMIDE, SEROTONIN, UBIDECARENONE, CORTICOTROPIN, ADENOSINE TRIPHOSPHATE, INSULIN PORK, various animal gland extracts, THIAMINE HYDROCHLORIDE, THYROID tissue, and numerous mineral and botanical ingredients—where adrenalin again functions as the active ingredient. In the MBM 11 Thyroid Adrenal preparation (containing IODINE 3 [hp_C] in 1 mL, PHYTOLACCA AMERICANA ROOT 3 [hp_C] in 1 mL, EPINEPHRINE 3 [hp_C] in 1 mL, CORTICOTROPIN 5 [hp_C] in 1 mL, SUS SCROFA THYROID 5 [hp_C] in 1 mL, ATROPA BELLADONNA 34 [hp_C] in 1 mL, PHOSPHORUS 55 [hp_C] in 1 mL, OYSTER SHELL CALCIUM CARBONATE 89 [hp_C] in 1 mL, SODIUM CHLORIDE 89 [hp_C] in 1 mL, NUTMEG 89 [hp_C] in 1 mL, DATURA STRAMONIUM 89 [hp_C] in 1 mL, and SILVER NITRATE 233 [hp_C] in 1 mL), adrenalin is likewise a principal active ingredient. Finally, adrenalin is the defining component of an “epinephrine 1 MG/ML Injectable Solution,” indicating its use as a parenteral medication. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that adrenalin functions both as an endogenous adrenal medulla hormone and as a pharmacologically active agent incorporated into diverse oral and injectable therapeutic preparations.

agent2_snippets.content: Multiple oral liquid formulations—identified for uses such as allergy management, neuro‑balance support, appetite and weight regulation, comprehensive health supplementation, and axis modulation—share adrenokortikotropní hormon (ACTH) as a declared active ingredient. In these products, ACTH is combined with a wide array of additional compounds, including catecholamine hormones (epinephrine, norepinephrine, serotonin, acetylcholine, glutamic acid, γ‑aminobutyric acid), other endocrine agents (cortisol‑related cortisone acetate, corticosterone, aldosterone, testosterone, melatonin, thiamine, niacinamide, insulin, levodopa, ubidecarenone), mineral salts (potassium chloride, sodium chloride, potassium dichromate, potassium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate, barium carbonate, iodine, phosphorus, arsenic trioxide, sulfur, silicon dioxide), botanical extracts (e.g., Ambrosia artemisiifolia, Arisaema triphyllum root, Bos taurus hypothalamus, Castanea sativa flower, Delphinium staphisagria seed, Fucus vesiculosus, Mucuna pruriens seed, Semecarpus anacardium juice, various seeds, roots, flowers, and juices), and animal‑derived tissues (sus scrofa nasal mucosa, pituitary gland, adrenal gland, hypothalamus, thyroid, parathyroid gland, pancreas). The concentrations of ACTH vary among the preparations, ranging from low‑dose units (e.g., 6 units) to higher levels (e.g., 12 units, 30 units), reflecting formulation differences for specific therapeutic goals. By incorporating ACTH alongside these diverse pharmacologic and nutraceutical components, the products aim to modulate the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis while providing ancillary effects such as anti‑inflammatory, metabolic, neuromodulatory, and mineral‑balancing actions, thereby supporting comprehensive patient or animal care.
